bibliographic.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, ‘Ave Maria,’ Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 27 (Neue Folge 15) (1904): 320–330. https://doi.org/10.1515/angl.1904.1904.27.320.
bibliographic.title: Ave Maria

entities.label: Heuser 1904 edition
notes: Section 1 occupies printed pages 320–323; Section 2 pages 323–325; Section 3 pages 326–329; and Section 4 pages 329–330.
The edition includes German comparative commentary, manuscript readings, five complete medieval verse items, and a Cambridge dateline with Heuser's signature.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1904, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: The 1904 edition, transcription, selection, and editorial commentary
notes: The author's verified death date was not established in this review, so life-plus-term status outside the United States remains unresolved.

rights.basis: The article's publication date is known, but a reliable death date for this Wilhelm Heuser was not established in the reviewed authority evidence; applicable terms and originality thresholds vary by jurisdiction.
rights.component: Any protectable selection, transcription choices, notes, or other editorial contribution

rights.basis: Internet Archive item metadata marks the volume possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and copyright-region US.
rights.component: The specific Internet Archive digital surrogate of Anglia volume 27
notes: The provider metadata is recorded as its assertion and does not replace independent jurisdiction-specific analysis.

Requestability does not guarantee that complete digitization is feasible or that supplied images may be redistributed.

entities.description: The manuscript witness for Heuser's Section 1 Middle English Ave Maria. The target poem is on fols. 92r–92v; the sole Digital Bodleian image is a non-overlapping slide of fols. 68v–69r.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Douce 126

notes: The Digital Bodleian record describes an English manuscript of the first half of the fifteenth century.
Heuser and DIMEV place the selected hymn on fols. 92r–92v.

rights.basis: The textual contents are medieval and far outside any U.S. copyright term.
rights.component: The anonymous medieval wording represented by all five texts, including the items in CUL MSS Gg.1.1 and Gg.4.32
notes: This statement concerns the historical wording, not modern photographs, scans, editions, transcriptions, metadata, or provider terms.

entities.description: The manuscript witness for Heuser's Section 2 Anglo-Norman Ave Maria. Direct inspection of CUDL images confirms the poem begins at the foot of fol. 264r, fills fol. 264v, and ends in the first two lines of fol. 265r.
entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Gg.1.1

notes: CUDL describes a trilingual compendium made in England, predominantly Anglo-Norman with Middle English and Latin.
The target fols. 264r–265r are written as a single-column verse sequence.

entities.description: The manuscript witness for Heuser's Section 3 four-line English salutation, the fifteen-stanza English hymn, and the Section 4 Latin hymn. Its multiple historical/current locator systems have not been fully reconciled.

holding.notes: No exact public CUL item catalogue page was verified. The linked Biblissima record is a scholarly aggregation, while Cambridge's repository supplies M. R. James's description.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.32

physicalDescription.format: Codex; double columns; 32–37 lines per page
notes: James describes many hands and calls the London connection strong while expressing doubt about an apparent Norwich ownership clue.
James locates the Latin Ave at legacy fol. 14v/current fol. 22v and lists the English Ave immediately after it; DIMEV gives f. 15v for the four-line salutation and ff. 23vb–24va for the long hymn.

access.provider: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

entities.description: The 2025 versioned DIMEV data release. Records 1728, 1731, and 1680 describe the three Middle English items represented in Heuser's selection and cite their witnesses and editions.
entities.label: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0
notes: Record 1728 corresponds to IMEV/NIMEV 1059; record 1731 to 1062; and record 1680 to 1024.
DIMEV's aabb classification for record 1728 conflicts with Heuser's statement that its quatrains use continuous rhyme and should be checked against fols. 92r–92v.

rights.basis: The Zenodo record identifies the release license as Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International.
rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data files and documentation

next_steps: Ask Cambridge University Library whether a current foliation concordance exists.
If images are supplied, align incipits, explicits, column boundaries, and every folio label against Heuser and DIMEV.
notes: A repository-confirmed crosswalk or complete facsimile is needed to give exact modern work portions for the two English items and the Latin hymn without conflating numbering systems.
openQuestions.question: How do Heuser's unfoliated/working references, M. R. James's legacy and bracketed foliation, and DIMEV's loci map onto the current leaves of CUL MS Gg.4.32?

next_steps: Ask Special Collections and Imaging Services about existing complete photography or a complete-object order.
Request fols. 92r–92v separately only if a complete-object copy is infeasible, while preserving the narrower coverage status.
notes: The public Digital Bodleian selection has no target overlap, so manuscript spelling, abbreviation, layout, and DIMEV's rhyme classification cannot yet be checked against images.
openQuestions.question: Can the Bodleian supply or identify a complete reproduction of MS Douce 126, including target fols. 92r–92v?

next_steps: Submit a CUL enquiry or imaging-form request for the complete codex and ask whether existing photography can reduce cost.
Obtain item-specific reuse terms before placing any supplied images in a public repository or publication workflow.
notes: The empty Biblissima IIIF Collection cannot resolve foliation, visual layout, completeness, or Heuser's manuscript-based editorial interventions.
openQuestions.question: Does Cambridge University Library hold an existing complete digital or microfilm surrogate of MS Gg.4.32 that is not publicly indexed?

next_steps: Inspect a manuscript facsimile or repository-supplied images of fols. 92r–92v.
Check Saupe's 1998 edition and any DIMEV revision history for an explanation or correction.
notes: The discovery metadata should not silently choose a rhyme pattern until Douce 126 fols. 92r–92v or a later critical discussion is checked.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV v1.0 classify record 1728 as aabb when Heuser describes continuous rhyme and the edited quatrains appear monorhymed?

next_steps: Consult a biographical authority or archival personnel record that clearly identifies the Anglist by profession and works.
Recalculate relevant non-U.S. terms only after identity and death date are securely established.
notes: The 1904 article is public domain in the United States, but life-plus-term calculations outside the United States remain incomplete.
openQuestions.question: What is the reliable death date of the Anglist Wilhelm Heuser who edited the 1904 article?

reviewNotes: The graph models five distinct medieval textual items inside one modern edited selection; it does not merge the article, its texts, their witnesses, digital encodings, or facsimiles.

Heuser's two Gg.4.32 section headings both cite fol. 14v after calling the manuscript unfoliated. M. R. James and DIMEV expose additional legacy/current locators, so each source's locator is preserved without a synthetic one-to-one crosswalk.

Access and rights statements apply only to the named entity or component. Public-domain medieval wording does not automatically determine rights or provider terms for modern photographs, metadata, editions, or encodings.
Negative whole-facsimile findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-13 and do not prove that no private, offline, or subsequently published reproduction exists.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00097, a keyed encoding of Wilhelm Heuser's complete 1904 Anglia article ‘Ave Maria.’ Heuser's comparative edited selection prints five anonymous medieval texts: a Middle English hymn from Bodleian MS Douce 126, an Anglo-Norman hymn from Cambridge University Library MS Gg.1.1, a four-line Middle English salutation and a longer Middle English hymn from CUL MS Gg.4.32, and a Latin hymn from the same CUL manuscript. The complete journal volume and complete CUDL image inventory for Gg.1.1 are cached; no complete public facsimile was verified for Douce 126 or Gg.4.32.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous medieval contributors
catalogSummary.date: Texts attested in fourteenth- and first-half-fifteenth-century English manuscripts; edited selection published 1904
catalogSummary.region: Medieval texts transmitted in English manuscript witnesses; article compiled in Cambridge and published in Halle an der Saale
catalogSummary.title: Ave Maria

catalogingSubject.label: Ave Maria
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The complete multilingual comparative article selected, transcribed, edited, and published by Wilhelm Heuser in 1904 and represented as CME00097: German commentary plus five distinct anonymous medieval verse items from three manuscripts. It is not a single medieval Ave Maria, any complete manuscript witness, the U-M digital encoding, the repository XML, or a facsimile.

contentParts.label: Heuser's editorial frame

locators.value: All DIV2/HEADNOTE and NOTE elements; DIV1 closing
320–330, interleaved with the five edited texts

notes: The embedded Middle English line ‘Be myn help and my fultum...’ is a quotation from a different Anglo-Norman poem discussed by Heuser, not a sixth cataloged item.
contentParts.summary: German introductions and comparative discussion for all four sections, eight manuscript/editorial notes, a one-line Middle English comparison quotation in the Section 2 headnote, manuscript and printed-page milestones, and the signed Cambridge closing.

contentParts.label: Hail Maiden of Maidens through Word Conceiving
notes: DIMEV record 1728 / IMEV-NIMEV 1059.
DIMEV's aabb rhyme classification conflicts with Heuser's continuous-rhyme description and remains unresolved pending manuscript-image review.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous Middle English hymn based on the Angelic Salutation, beginning ‘Hayle mayden of maydyns thorȝht worde consaywyng’; seventeen long quatrains address Mary through the successive words of the Ave Maria.

contentParts.label: Ave tresduce Marie
notes: The final two poem lines occupy the top of fol. 265r; the same leaf continues with the next manuscript text.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous Anglo-Norman hymn of sixteen monorhyme quatrains, each stanza beginning with a successive word of the Angelic Salutation. Heuser prints the complete text after noting that Paul Meyer had previously printed only its first two lines.

contentParts.label: Hail Mary Full of Grace / God Is with Thee in Every Place
notes: DIMEV record 1731 / IMEV-NIMEV 1062 also records variant copies in Cambridge, Emmanuel College MS 27 and BL Cotton Cleopatra B VI; Heuser's selected text is the Gg.4.32 copy.
No synthetic crosswalk is asserted between Heuser's and DIMEV's folio references.
contentParts.summary: A four-line anonymous Middle English Ave Maria that serves as the heading or acrostic key to the following fifteen-stanza hymn.

contentParts.label: Hail and Holy Ay Be Thy Name

locators.value: Heuser working sequence: fols. 14v–15v; DIMEV: ff. 23vb–24va

notes: DIMEV record 1680 / IMEV-NIMEV 1024; rhyme pattern abababab.
The foliation systems remain unreconciled without current manuscript images or repository guidance.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous Middle English hymn to the Virgin in fifteen eight-line stanzas. Each stanza begins with a successive word from the preceding four-line salutation, producing a type of acrostic based on the Angelic Salutation.

contentParts.label: Ave, Dei Genitrix, Semper Deo Grata

locators.value: Heuser: fol. 14v; M. R. James: legacy fol. 14v / bracketed current fol. 22v at opening

notes: The reviewed sources secure the opening and adjacency but do not supply a verified current end folio.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous Latin Ave Maria in twelve quatrains. Heuser observes that its final line in each stanza shifts from the hymn's trochaic rhythm toward hexameter and that it immediately precedes the English material in Gg.4.32.

dateStatements.display: Compiled by Wilhelm Heuser no later than 1904
notes: This dates the modern edited selection, not the composition of its medieval texts.

assertion.notes: DIMEV records only this witness for record 1728; the date is manuscript production, not a proved composition date.

dateStatements.display: Attested in Bodleian MS Douce 126, produced in the first half of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: This is the represented witness's production range; the hymn's composition date and any earlier witnesses remain unknown.

dateStatements.display: Attested in CUL MS Gg.1.1, produced in the first half of the fourteenth century after 1307

assertion.notes: The type remains unknown because the reviewed evidence does not prove that Gg.4.32 is the first surviving attestation of the four-line salutation, which DIMEV also records in other witnesses.

dateStatements.display: The represented Gg.4.32 copies are in a manuscript dated broadly to the fourteenth century; their composition dates and relative priority are unknown

extent.description: At the modeled encoded-unit boundary, 67 counted items comprise six editorial prose paragraphs and 61 verse groups, organized as one noncontiguous editorial-frame part and five distinct medieval works. The whole item contains 305 verse-line elements: 304 core lines in the five works (17/68, 16/64, 1/4, 15/120, and 12/48) plus one Middle English comparison line quoted inside the German Section 2 headnote. The XML has only 60 LG because the four-line salutation is an EPIGRAPH/Q rather than LG. The editorial quotation is reported separately in the frame metrics and must not inflate any direct medieval work's line count.

formStatements.description: The complete edited selection intentionally combines substantial German prose commentary with five verse texts.
German introductions and comparative commentary, with brief quoted verse and footnotes.
All five medieval items are versified; the Section 4 Latin prose salutation serves as an epigraph to its twelve quatrains.

assertion.notes: DIMEV explicitly classifies the Middle English items as hymns to the Virgin; the Anglo-Norman and Latin items are parallel versified Ave Maria texts.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: A separate Middle English line quoted in the Section 2 German headnote is editorial paratext, not a sixth medieval item.

languageStatements.label: Anglo-Norman French
notes: The repository XML's root language declaration omits this language even though the complete Section 2 text is Anglo-Norman.

The article's medieval item count is five, not four: the four-line Middle English salutation in Section 3 and the following fifteen-stanza hymn are separately indexed works even though Heuser presents them together.
The raw XML L count is 305, while the five core medieval items total 304 verse lines; the extra line is a Middle English quotation embedded in Heuser's German Section 2 headnote.
The preferred title and whole-work mixed form describe the cataloged edited selection. Each five medieval content parts remains verse-only and anonymous.
Anglo-Norman code xno is added from direct evidence even though the source XML's TEXT/@LANG declares only Middle English, German, and Latin.

next_steps: Review later critical editions and language studies for each independently identified text.
Keep any dialect localization separate from place of composition and manuscript production.
notes: The current date and region summary must remain witness- and edition-qualified rather than presenting manuscript production as composition.
openQuestions.question: When and where were the five anonymous medieval texts composed, as distinct from the production dates and locations of their represented witnesses?

next_steps: Ask Cambridge University Library for a foliation concordance or complete reproduction.
Align incipits, explicits, columns, and folio labels directly if images become available.
notes: Exact current folio and canvas ranges for three of the five items cannot be asserted until a repository crosswalk or complete facsimile is available.
openQuestions.question: How do Heuser's working foliation, M. R. James's legacy/bracketed references, and DIMEV's loci for the Gg.4.32 items map to the current manuscript leaves?

next_steps: Request or locate images of fols. 92r–92v.
Consult Saupe's 1998 edition and DIMEV revision history.
notes: No rhyme scheme is exposed as normalized metadata until the conflict is resolved.
openQuestions.question: Does direct inspection of Douce 126 fols. 92r–92v support Heuser's continuous-rhyme description or DIMEV's aabb classification for record 1728?

assertion.notes: The signed article closes with a Cambridge dateline; this supports the place of Heuser's editorial work, not the medieval works' composition.
This association concerns the manuscript tradition and does not assign England as the place of composition for every text.

scope.description: Transmission in manuscript witnesses produced or held in England

assertion.notes: No reviewed source securely attributes any of the five medieval items to a named historical author.

notes: The collective display summarizes multiple anonymous creators or transmitters; it does not assert common authorship.

scope.description: Selection and comparative organization of five items from three manuscript witnesses
German introductions, comparative argument, manuscript notes, and signed closing

summaries.value: Wilhelm Heuser's 1904 article brings together five anonymous medieval expansions of the Ave Maria from three manuscripts: a seventeen-quatrain Middle English hymn from Bodleian MS Douce 126; a sixteen-quatrain Anglo-Norman hymn from CUL MS Gg.1.1; a four-line Middle English salutation and a fifteen-stanza Middle English hymn from CUL MS Gg.4.32; and a twelve-quatrain Latin hymn that immediately precedes the English material in Gg.4.32. German introductions compare their structure, rhyme, language, and possible relationships, while local notes report manuscript readings and expansions.

Section 1: ‘Hayle mayden of maydyns thorȝht worde consaywyng’ (68 lines). Section 2: ‘Ave tresduce Marie’ (64 lines), preceded by German comparison and one quoted Middle English line from another poem. Section 3: ‘Heil Marie, ful of grace’ (4 lines) followed by ‘Heil and holi ay be þi name’ (120 lines). Section 4: ‘Ave, dei genitrix, semper deo grata’ (48 lines).

notes: This is Heuser's publication title for the comparative selection, not a manuscript-supplied collective title for all five medieval texts.

assertion.notes: A project-supplied clarification for discovery, not a published alternate title.

titles.value: Ave Maria: Five Medieval Texts Edited by Wilhelm Heuser

work.author: Anonymous medieval contributors
work.dateDisplay: Texts attested in fourteenth- and first-half-fifteenth-century English manuscripts; edited selection published 1904
work.regionDisplay: Medieval texts transmitted in English manuscript witnesses; article compiled in Cambridge and published in Halle an der Saale
work.summary: Wilhelm Heuser's 1904 article brings together five anonymous medieval expansions of the Ave Maria from three manuscripts: a seventeen-quatrain Middle English hymn from Bodleian MS Douce 126; a sixteen-quatrain Anglo-Norman hymn from CUL MS Gg.1.1; a four-line Middle English salutation and a fifteen-stanza Middle English hymn from CUL MS Gg.4.32; and a twelve-quatrain Latin hymn that immediately precedes the English material in Gg.4.32. German introductions compare their structure, rhyme, language, and possible relationships, while local notes report manuscript readings and expansions.
